evidence_quotes.quote: First off, I greatly support limiting dark patterns.
One dark pattern I would like to highlight in detail is the use of virtual currencies that hide the true cost of something.
One thing I think is missing from the proposal is that I think the concept of ownership of digital products needs to be regulated.

paragraphs.text: First off, I greatly support limiting dark patterns. I think they are doing far more damage than we give them credit for. I do expect this to become an arms race, due to the massive amounts of money involved. As part of these regulations, a commission should be created that maintains a list of what counts as a dark pattern. One dark pattern I would like to highlight in detail is the use of virtual currencies that hide the true cost of something. A plethora of mobile games rely on this. One thing I think is missing from the proposal is that I think the concept of ownership of digital products needs to be regulated. It happens far too often that services on which products have been purchased, or individual products that are bought online, are shut down with little notice. When this happens, I as a consumer have no recourse, as the terms of these services are given to me in a "take it or leave it" kind of manner, which explicitly allow a company to take these purchases away from me. I believe that it is time to codify the concept of owning a digital product, and that a company must take steps to ensure that I will be able to use the product I bought in the future. I supported the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative for this reason, but I personally believe that this should also apply to purchases of movies, music, ebooks, and other digital products. In short: if I buy a digital copy of something online, I'd like that to count as owning that product, and the law requiring companies to ensure that I can keep on using that product. Another practice that I think should be regulated more is how companies are allowed to change their terms and conditions in a one-sided manner. Especially for large platforms you sometimes are required to use these services to get by in daily life. That does not make their use optional, yet you are required to accept their conditions if and when they decide to update these. I'd like to see some protections for consumers in this direction. For example, a requirement for companies to explain what the changes mean in plain language, rather than the commonly used legal mumbo jumbo, or requiring a company to honour an earlier version of the terms and conditions that a user originally agreed to.
